name: Tessen Vael

psychologicalProfile.wound: Tessen survived a patrol disaster and learned the empire blames scouts faster than bad intelligence.
psychologicalProfile.desire: Remain beholden to no chain of command and keep the Rivenmarch navigable on his own terms.
psychologicalProfile.fear: Being drawn back into someone else's mission and losing more people to lies from above.
psychologicalProfile.lie: Refusing to belong is the same as being free.
psychologicalProfile.ghost: A border patrol he guided was sent into a trap, then blamed on terrain error after the dead were counted.
psychologicalProfile.need: To choose loyalty again as consent rather than obedience.

arc.startState: A ghost of the Legion who trusts trails more than people.
arc.endState: A guide who reclaims part of his old oath by helping the right commander.

turningPoints.event: Reads Kaelith's movement as old Ashborne path-work.
Agrees to guide only after Kaelith offers real truth.

voiceFingerprint.vocabularyLevel: Plain border vernacular stripped to essentials. Tessen speaks as though words are rations — you use what you need and waste nothing. His vocabulary comes from terrain, weather, and animal behavior: 'ridge,' 'runoff,' 'game trail,' 'wind-shift,' 'false ground.' He names things by what they do, not what they are: a cliff is 'a fall,' a marsh is 'slow death,' a clear path is 'a gift or a trap.' He rarely reaches for abstractions and tends not to name emotions directly.
voiceFingerprint.sentenceLength: Short to clipped as default. Instruction-shaped: subject-verb or imperative. 'Step where I step.' 'Wind's shifting.' 'Not that way.' Longer sentences emerge only when explaining terrain danger, and even then they are chains of short clauses: 'The ground looks solid but it shifted last season and anything built on the old line is sitting on air.' He seldom elaborates unless the elaboration prevents death.
voiceFingerprint.metaphorDensity: Low, drawn from weather, terrain, and animal sign. A trustworthy person is 'solid ground.' A liar 'reads like false trail.' Danger 'smells like still water.' He gravitates away from garden (Thalen's domain), architecture (Kaelith's domain), water-as-infiltration (Lirien's domain), medical (Marin's domain), and scholarly metaphors, though occasional natural crossover is expected — rigid domain walls create artificiality. His world is the land — and the land does not explain itself.
voiceFingerprint.emotionalRegister: Dry, suspicious, and intermittently tender. His default register is wariness — every person is terrain he hasn't verified. Warmth emerges obliquely: he expresses care through practical action (sharing food, adjusting someone's pack) rather than words. When genuinely moved, he goes silent rather than eloquent.
voiceFingerprint.thoughtPatterns: Environmental and conditional. Tessen thinks in if-then chains rooted in terrain: 'If the wind shifts, then the scent carries. If the scent carries, then we're downwind of the patrol.' Most thoughts run as trail assessments. He applies this pattern to people too — reading body language as animal sign, treating trust as ground you test before you cross.
voiceFingerprint.dialectMarkers: Rivenmarch freeholder speech: avoids titles and honorifics, chosen names only. Landmark directions instead of compass points: 'toward the split oak,' 'past where the cairn was.' Practical profanity — brief, functional, not performative. He drops subjects from sentences: 'Shifting' instead of 'The wind is shifting.'
voiceFingerprint.humorStyle: Rare, deadpan, and terrain-based. His jokes are so dry they might not be jokes: 'Last three people who took that path came back. Two of them on foot.' The humor functions as warning dressed in understatement. He does not laugh.
voiceFingerprint.attentionPriority: What Tessen notices first: (1) Ground — is this surface trustworthy? (2) Wind — what does it carry, what does it reveal? (3) Silence — what should be making sound and isn't? (4) People's feet — how they walk tells him more than what they say.
voiceFingerprint.deflectionPattern: Changes the subject to immediate terrain. Asked about his past: 'Ground's soft here. Watch your step.' Asked about loyalty: 'Trail forks ahead. Left is faster, right is safer.' He answers emotional questions with environmental facts, and the facts are always relevant enough that the deflection succeeds.
voiceFingerprint.stressResponse: Words drop to single-word commands. 'Down.' 'Still.' 'Now.' Under extreme stress he goes entirely silent and communicates through gesture — a raised fist, a pointed finger, a head shake. Speech returns only after the danger passes, and it returns with one extra sentence of explanation that is the closest Tessen comes to apology.

sensorySig.signature: wind on stone and birdless silence

relationships.dynamicArc: Trust hinges on whether Kaelith offers honesty rather than rank.
He distrusts Lirien until she adapts to the land instead of managing it.
Erys represents the loyalty Tessen walked away from.
Marin and Tessen trade practical trust outside imperial structures.

physicalDescription.distinguishingFeatures: sun-scoured skin and an old patrol scar

agency: Tessen determines whether the protagonists can move through the Rivenmarch with any realistic chance of survival. His decision to help is contingent, active, and strategically decisive.

dialoguePatterns.initiationStyle: Doesn't initiate unless necessary. When he must: a single factual statement about the environment. 'Weather's turning.' 'Something moved up the ridge.' He trusts others to understand the implication.
dialoguePatterns.responseStyle: Answers questions with the minimum viable information. 'How far?' 'Half a day.' 'Is it safe?' 'Safer than here.' His responses are compressed to the point where listeners must unpack the meaning themselves.
dialoguePatterns.underPressure: Single-word imperatives. All grammar falls away. 'Move.' 'Stop.' 'Quiet.' If pressed to explain: one terse clause. 'Because the ground is about to give.' Then silence.
verbalTics: Drops sentence subjects: 'Shifting' not 'The wind is shifting'
Uses 'reads like' for assessments: 'Reads like false ground'
Says 'not that way' instead of explaining why
Refers to distances by landmarks, never measurements
Begins rare longer statements with 'Here's the thing about —'
